name: Bodhayana
dates_notes: Identity disputed; cited by Rāmānuja as the Vṛttikāra whose lost commentary Rāmānuja's school claims as its authority.
dating_methodology: Dating Bodhāyana is exceptionally constrained because the figure is at the intersection of three identity-questions: (i) is he the *Vṛttikāra* on the *Brahma-Sūtra* whom Rāmānuja invokes in the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* preface; (ii) is he the *Vṛttikāra* on Jaimini's *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* whom Śabara summarizes; (iii) is he distinct from or identical with Upavarṣa. Each identification is contested. Terminus ante quem: he is presupposed by the Śrīvaiṣṇava *sampradāya* claim transmitted through Nāthamuni (ninth century) and explicitly invoked by Rāmānuja (eleventh-twelfth century); he must therefore be earlier than the early ninth century at the latest, and probably substantially earlier if the lost *Vṛtti* circulated as authoritative for several generations before Nāthamuni. Terminus post quem: the *Brahma-Sūtra-Vṛtti* form (running commentary on Bādarāyaṇa) presupposes the redacted *Brahma-Sūtra* (circa 200–100 BCE) and the established Mīmāṃsā analytical apparatus, suggesting a date no earlier than the second century CE. The window 300–500 CE follows van Buitenen's reconstruction (*Rāmānuja's Vedārthasaṃgraha*, 1956, introduction) and is broadly accepted by Carman (*The Theology of Rāmānuja*, 1974). Hiriyanna (*Outlines of Indian Philosophy*, 1932) prefers a slightly earlier window (200–400 CE) on the grounds of Bodhāyana's apparent priority to the developed Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā commentarial tradition. Note (AI-generated audit): no autograph, no manuscript, no biographical record. The Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition's identification of Bodhāyana as the founding *ācārya* of its lineage is doctrinally invested and may inflate his philosophical proximity to Viśiṣṭādvaita; the conservative position, taken here, is that *some* pre-Śaṅkara *Brahma-Sūtra-Vṛtti* existed, that Rāmānuja appeals to it as authority, and that the actual textual content cannot be reconstructed beyond what Rāmānuja's citations preserve.
core_thesis:
What can be philosophically engaged in Bodhāyana is what Rāmānuja's *Śrī-Bhāṣya* (preface, *mukha-bhūmikā*) and *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* (§§133, 142) preserve as the *vṛtti-kāra*-vacanas — sentences attributed to him by name. The position they articulate is that Brahman is the inner self of all beings (*sarvāntaryāmin*) (so the citation at *Śrī-Bhāṣya* preface; cf. *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad* 3.7 *antaryāmin-brāhmaṇa* as the canonical scriptural locus), and that the *jīva* is a real *aṃśa* (part-portion) of Brahman, dependent on Brahman as a mode (*prakāra*) is dependent on the substance it modifies (the citation at *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §§133–142, attributing this to the *vṛttikāra*; cf. [*Brahma-Sūtra* 2.3.43](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/2.3.43) *aṃśo nānāvyapadeśāt*). The doctrine is structurally proto-Viśiṣṭādvaita rather than a developed Viśiṣṭādvaita: the *aṃśa*-relation is asserted, the *prakāra*-relation is implicit in the inner-controllership claim, but the developed *śarīra-śarīrin* metaphysics that Rāmānuja systematizes ([*Śrī-Bhāṣya* 2.1.9](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/2.1.9); *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* §76) is not preserved in the Bodhāyana fragments. The fragments equally support a *bhedābheda* reading. The *aṃśa*-thesis (the *jīva* is a real part of Brahman) is shared with Bhāskara ([*Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* on 2.3.43](cite://bhaskara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.3.43)) and Nimbārka ([*Vedānta-Pārijāta-Saurabha* on 2.3.43](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.43)); the *antaryāmin*-thesis (Brahman is the inner self of all) is shared with Advaita (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.2.18) as well as Viśiṣṭādvaita. Whether the historical Bodhāyana committed to a position structurally distinct from Bhāskara's *bhedābheda* — which would make his recovery as a Viśiṣṭādvaita ancestor philologically secure — cannot be settled from the surviving citation-evidence (so van Buitenen, *Rāmānuja's Vedārthasaṃgraha*, 1956, intro.).

The philosophical question raised by Rāmānuja's appeal to Bodhāyana is therefore methodological as well as doctrinal. Rāmānuja, in the *mukha-bhūmikā* of the *Śrī-Bhāṣya*, writes: *bhagavad-bodhāyana-kṛtāṃ vistīrṇāṃ brahma-sūtra-vṛttiṃ pūrvācāryāḥ saṃcikṣipuḥ; tan-mata-anusāreṇa sūtrākṣarāṇi vyākhyāsyante* — 'former teachers abridged the extensive *Brahma-Sūtra-Vṛtti* of Bhagavān Bodhāyana; the sūtras will here be expounded in accordance with his view.' The sentence does two things at once. First, it claims a continuous transmission: an extensive Bodhāyana commentary, abridged by intermediate teachers, recoverable in summary by Rāmānuja's own work. Second, it positions Rāmānuja's exposition as derivative from Bodhāyana, not original to himself. The combined effect is to ground Viśiṣṭādvaita in a pre-Śaṅkara textual authority that Śaṅkara himself ignored. The argument is dialectically powerful: if Bodhāyana antedates Śaṅkara and read the sūtras in the qualified-non-dual sense, then Śaṅkara's strict-non-difference reading is the innovation, not the inheritance. The argument is also entirely dependent on the authenticity and content of the lost *Vṛtti*, neither of which is independently verifiable.

The scholarly question is whether anything stronger than 'Rāmānuja appeals to Bodhāyana' can be said. The answer, on the surviving evidence, is: a small amount. The fragments Rāmānuja and Sudarśana Sūri quote do affirm Brahman as inner self of all and the *jīva* as a real *aṃśa*; this much is compatible with several pre-Śaṅkara positions and probably reflects an actual textual source. The fragments do not, however, articulate the developed *śarīra-śarīrin* analogy or the *apṛthak-siddhi* relation that distinguishes mature Viśiṣṭādvaita from generic *bhedābheda*. The honest reconstruction is that Bodhāyana taught a real-aṃśa, inner-controller Vedānta in a form sufficiently close to Viśiṣṭādvaita that Rāmānuja could plausibly claim him, and sufficiently general that other *bhedābheda* schools could plausibly contest the claim. The lost *Vṛtti* probably existed; what it contained beyond the cited fragments is not recoverable.

The identity question — whether Bodhāyana is the same as Upavarṣa, or distinct — is taken up at the Upavarṣa entry. The traditional Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā identification (Śabara's *Vṛttikāra* = Upavarṣa = Bodhāyana) is not philologically settled; van Buitenen and Carman treat the three as probably distinct historical figures who became identified in later doxographic memory. Nothing in Rāmānuja's preserved citations of Bodhāyana matches the Mīmāṃsā *Vṛttikāra* fragments preserved in Śabara, which weighs against full identification.

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Sutra-Vrtti (lost)
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Work is lost; preserved only as fragments cited in Rāmānuja's Śrī-Bhāṣya, with substantial scholarly dispute over whether the cited material accurately reflects a single historical author.
engaged_works.summary: The *Brahma-Sūtra-Vṛtti* attributed to Bodhāyana is lost. Its existence and authority are asserted by Rāmānuja in the *mukha-bhūmikā* of the *Śrī-Bhāṣya*: *bhagavad-bodhāyana-kṛtāṃ vistīrṇāṃ brahma-sūtra-vṛttiṃ pūrvācāryāḥ saṃcikṣipuḥ; tan-mata-anusāreṇa sūtrākṣarāṇi vyākhyāsyante* — 'former teachers abridged the extensive Brahma-Sūtra-Vṛtti of Bhagavān Bodhāyana; the sūtras will here be expounded in accordance with his view.' Rāmānuja thereby grounds the entire Viśiṣṭādvaita reading of the sūtras in a pre-Śaṅkara textual authority. The fragments preserved by name (in the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* itself, in the *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha*, and in later Śrīvaiṣṇava commentaries by Sudarśana Sūri and Vedānta Deśika) affirm two doctrinal commitments: that Brahman is the inner self of all beings (*sarvāntaryāmin*), and that the *jīva* is a real *aṃśa* of Brahman, dependent on Brahman as a mode is dependent on its substance. These commitments are compatible with developed Viśiṣṭādvaita but also with *bhedābheda*; the distinguishing *śarīra-śarīrin* and *apṛthak-siddhi* analyses of Rāmānuja are not preserved in the Bodhāyana fragments themselves. The work's actual scope, date, and identity as a *Brahma-Sūtra* (rather than Mīmāṃsā) commentary are scholarly disputes (van Buitenen, *Rāmānuja's Vedārthasaṃgraha*, 1956; Carman, *The Theology of Rāmānuja*, 1974). Modern philology accepts that some pre-Śaṅkara *Vṛttikāra* tradition existed but cannot reconstruct its content beyond what Rāmānuja preserves. Whether Bodhāyana is the same as Upavarṣa or the Mīmāṃsā-*Vṛttikāra* whom Śabara summarizes is unsettled; the cited fragments do not match across the two traditions, weighing against identification.

quoted_by.locus: RVas_92 (the *bodhāyana-ṭaṅka-draviḍa-guhadeva-kapardi-bhāruci* list invoking Bodhāyana as foremost of the *purātana-vyākhyātṛ*-lineage)
quoted_by.what_quoted: Bodhāyana invoked as the founding pre-Śaṅkara *vyākhyātṛ* whose Vedānta-reading authorises Rāmānuja's path.
*bhagavad-bodhāyana-kṛtāṃ vistīrṇāṃ brahma-sūtra-vṛttiṃ pūrvācāryāḥ saṃcikṣipuḥ; tan-mata-anusāreṇa sūtrākṣarāṇi vyākhyāsyante* — Rāmānuja's claim of inheritance from Bodhāyana's lost Vṛtti.
quoted_by.on_disk_note: Sribhasya part 1 (Karmakar 1959 ed.) is on disk; OCR quality is poor and the *mukha-bhūmikā* must be consulted in the PDF rather than via text-search. The Bodhāyana citation in the *Vedārtha-Saṅgraha* is text-searchable on disk and confirms the reference.
